options.text: Having read the report, she outlined her recommendations.
The bridge built in 1902 still carries heavy traffic.
Walking past the stadium, the cheering of the crowd could be heard.
Not knowing what to expect, the delegates arrived early.

questions.explanation: Option (c) is a dangling participle: "Walking past the stadium" implies the cheering was walking — impossible. The subject of the main clause (the cheering of the crowd) cannot perform the walking. Correct version: Walking past the stadium, **the delegates** could hear the crowd cheering. Options (a), (b), and (d) are all correctly formed participle clauses with matching subjects.

questions.prompt: Choose the best reduction for a formal report: "The evidence was analysed by the forensic team. They then published their findings."

options.text: Analysing the evidence, their findings were published.
The forensic team, having analysed the evidence, published their findings.
Analysed by the forensic team, they published their findings.
The forensic team analysing the evidence, their findings were published.

questions.explanation: Option (b) is correct: the forensic team first analysed (having + past participle — prior action), then published. Subject is consistent (the forensic team). Option (a) is a dangling participle — "their findings" cannot analyse. Option (c) is also dangling — "they" (the team) are not the ones being analysed. Option (d) is an absolute participle clause (different subject) — possible in some contexts but awkward and ambiguous here.

questions.prompt: Which reduction correctly preserves the meaning of: "The children who were left unsupervised caused significant damage"?

options.text: The children leaving unsupervised caused significant damage.
The children left unsupervised caused significant damage.
The children having left unsupervised caused significant damage.
The children to leave unsupervised caused significant damage.

questions.explanation: "Who were left unsupervised" is a passive relative clause → past participle: the children **left** unsupervised. The past participle retains the passive meaning (they were left, not they left). Option (a) "leaving" (active present participle) implies the children left of their own accord — different meaning. Option (c) "having left" uses the perfect participle — awkward and changes the nuance. Option (d) is a to-infinitive, changing the meaning further.

questions.prompt: Identify the function of the participle clause in: "The treaty, signed by all parties in 1945, reshaped the post-war world."

options.text: Reduced passive relative clause giving additional information about the treaty
Perfect participle clause expressing a prior action
Present participle clause expressing simultaneous action
Adverbial past participle clause expressing reason

questions.explanation: "Signed by all parties in 1945" is a reduced non-defining relative clause: it adds information about the treaty (which was signed by all parties in 1945). It is non-defining (set off by commas). Option (c) is wrong — "signed" is not an -ing form. Option (b) is wrong — the perfect participle uses "having + pp", not a bare past participle. Option (d) is wrong — the clause modifies the noun (the treaty), not the whole main verb action.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct participle form: "___ in the 15th century, the palace contains priceless works of art."

options.text: Building
Being built
Having built
Built

questions.explanation: "Built in the 15th century" is a past participle adverbial clause expressing time/concession — meaning "(although it was) built in the 15th century". The past participle carries the passive sense. Option (a) "Building" is active — implies the palace is building something. Option (c) "Having built" implies the palace actively built something in the past. Option (b) "Being built" is passive continuous — implies it is currently under construction, contradicting "15th century".

questions.prompt:
Read: "The investigation concluded. The jury delivered a unanimous verdict."
Which single sentence BEST preserves both the sequence and the formal register?

options.text: The investigation concluding, the jury delivered a unanimous verdict.
Concluding the investigation, a unanimous verdict was delivered.
Having concluded the investigation, the jury delivered a unanimous verdict.
The jury concluded the investigation, delivering a unanimous verdict.

questions.explanation: The investigation concluded FIRST, then the verdict was delivered. Use having + past participle for the prior action: Having concluded the investigation, the jury delivered a unanimous verdict. The subject (the jury) is consistent. Option (a) uses present participle "concluding" — implies both actions are simultaneous. Option (b) is a dangling participle — "a unanimous verdict" cannot conclude the investigation. Option (d) changes the sentence structure so the jury concluded the investigation AND delivered the verdict simultaneously as narrative events — alters the sequence emphasis.
